temples.name: Ambaji Temple
temples.description: Ambaji Temple in Banaskantha district, Gujarat, enshrines one of the 51 Shakti Peethas—here the heart (or, in some traditions, the right arm) of Sati fell to earth. Uniquely, there is no idol in the sanctum; the goddess is represented by a sacred Vishwa Yantra (geometric diagram) draped in a fabric mask, making it one of the most abstract and mystically charged Shakti shrines. The white marble temple complex lies in the Aravalli hills and draws millions each year, with the largest surge during the Bhadarvi Poonam fair in August/September when 1.5 million pilgrims arrive overnight.
temples.shortDescription: Shakti Peetha in Gujarat's Aravalli hills with no idol—the goddess is a sacred yantra draped in cloth. One of Gujarat's most visited shrines with 1.5 million at Bhadarvi Poonam.

facts.dresscode: Traditional attire required; no non-Hindus in sanctum

temples.name: Amba Mata Temple
temples.description: The Ambika Mata Temple at Jagat, Rajasthan, is a 10th-century masterpiece of the Pratihara-Gurjara school — often called the Khajuraho of Rajasthan for its extraordinarily carved exterior panels depicting amorous couples, celestial beings, and battle scenes alongside devotional imagery. Built around 961 CE under the Paramara dynasty, the sanctum enshrines Ambika (the mother form of the goddess) with subsidiary shrines to Ganesha and the Sapta Matrikas. Though smaller than the great Khajuraho temples, the sculptural quality rivals them, and the temple's remote setting in the Aravalli foothills has preserved both stone and sanctity.
temples.shortDescription: A 10th-century Pratihara-Gurjara temple renowned for Khajuraho-style erotic and celestial carvings, tucked away in the Rajasthan hills.

temples.name: Attahas Shakti Peetha
temples.description: The Attahas Shakti Peetha at Labhpur, Birbhum, marks where the lower lip of Sati fell — giving the site its name (Atta-has: "great laughter", Sati's last expression). The presiding goddess Phullara (or Ugratara) is revered in the Shakta tantric tradition. The massive goddess is said to be 15–18 feet wide at the lip. December is peak pilgrimage season, when thousands visit the temple for rituals and festivals. The rural Bengal setting adds to the atmosphere of this ancient and deeply charged sacred site.
temples.shortDescription: Birbhum Shakti Peetha where Sati's lower lip fell — Phullara devi, goddess of great laughter, revered in the tantric tradition.

temples.name: Sabarimala Temple
temples.description: The Sabarimala Sree Dharma Sastha Temple (Malayalam: ശബരിമല ശ്രീ ധർമ്മ ശാസ്താ ക്ഷേത്രം) (Malayalam pronunciation: [ʃabəɾimala]) is a Hindu temple dedicated to Lord Sree Dharma Sastha where the deity is worshipped as Lord  Ayyappan, the son of the deities Shiva and Mohini (female avatar of the god Vishnu), and is situated atop the Sabarimala hill in Pathanamthitta district of Kerala, India. The temple is surrounded by 18 hills in the Periyar Tiger Reserve. It is one of the largest annual pilgrimage sites in the world, with an estimate of over 10 to 15 million devotees visiting every year.

temples.name: Bahula Shakti Peetha
temples.description: The Bahula Temple at Ketugram in Purba Bardhaman, West Bengal, enshrines Goddess Bahula — a gentle form of Parvati — at the spot where the left arm of Sati fell, according to Shakta tradition. One of the 51 Shakti Peethas, the temple draws pilgrims seeking blessings for family welfare and fertility. The presiding Bhairava is Vamsanara. The location near the Ajay River lends this modest but deeply sacred pilgrimage site a serene, timeless quality.
temples.shortDescription: West Bengal Shakti Peetha where Sati's left arm fell — gentle goddess Bahula (Parvati) presides at Ketugram on the Ajay River.

temples.name: Bakreshwar Shakti Peetha
temples.description: The Bakreshwar temple complex in Birbhum, West Bengal, marks the Shakti Peetha where the mid-forehead of Sati fell. The presiding goddess Mahishmardini (Durga) is paired with the Bhairava Vakranath. Uniquely, Bakreshwar has 11 natural hot springs of varying temperatures — some near boiling — considered medicinal and sacred. Pilgrims bathe in the springs before darshan, linking Shakti energy with the earth's volcanic heat. The complex of multiple temples, hot springs and natural pools makes Bakreshwar one of West Bengal's most distinctive pilgrim sites.
temples.shortDescription: Birbhum Shakti Peetha known for 11 sacred hot springs — Mahishmardini Durga is worshipped here where Sati's forehead fell.

temples.name: Tulja Bhavani Temple
temples.description: Shree Tulaja Bhavani Temple (Marathi: श्री तुळजाभवानी मंदिर), is a 12th century CE Hindu temple dedicated to goddess Bhavani. It was built in 12th century CE by Mahamandaleshwara Māradadeva of the Kadamb dynasty. Considered as one of the 51 Shakti Pithas, it is located on the banks of Mandakini River and Bori Dam in Yamunachala Hill of Balaghat Range of Tuljapur, which is 45 km northeast of Solapur, in Dharashiv district of Maharashtra in India.

temples.name: Bhawani Temple (Chandranath Peetha)
temples.description: Rising dramatically above Sitakunda's plains, Chandranath Temple crowns a forested hilltop 315 metres above sea level in southeastern Bangladesh. It is doubly sacred — a Shakti Peetha where the right arm of Goddess Sati fell, and a Shaiva seat where Shiva is worshipped as Chandranath. References appear in the medieval text Nigamkalpataru, and the Tripura kingdom endowed the temple nearly 800 years ago. Every spring, over a million pilgrims ascend its stone steps for the Shiva Chaturdashi fair, one of Bangladesh's largest Hindu gatherings.
temples.shortDescription: A hilltop Shakti Peetha in Sitakunda, Bangladesh — doubly sacred as the spot where Sati's right arm fell and as a revered Shaiva seat of Chandranath.

festivals.name: Shiva Chaturdashi Fair
festivals.description: A 10-day pilgrimage festival drawing over a million devotees to climb the hill; one of the largest Hindu gatherings in Bangladesh, culminating on Dol Purnima.

temples.name: Srisailam Bhramaramba Temple
temples.description: Deep in the Nallamala forest on the banks of the Krishna River, Bhramaramba — 'Mother of Bees' — is enshrined as one of the 18 Maha Shakti Peethas, where Sati's throat is believed to have fallen. This fierce form of Parvati takes her name from the legend in which she manifested as a swarm of black bees to slay the demon Arunasura. Sharing a walled complex with the Mallikarjuna Jyotirlinga, Srisailam is uniquely the only site in India that is simultaneously a Jyotirlinga and an 18 Maha Shakti Peetha.
temples.shortDescription: A forest Shakti Peetha where Parvati as Bhramaramba — 'Mother of Bees' — is worshipped where Sati's throat fell; the only site in India that is both a Jyotirlinga and a Maha Shakti Peetha.

temples.name: Biraja Temple (Shakti Peetha)
temples.description: The Biraja Temple at Jajpur in Odisha is one of the most venerated of the 51 Shakti Peethas — tradition holds that the navel (nabhi) of Sati fell here, making it a seat of supreme creative power. The presiding goddess Viraja (a fierce form of Durga) is worshipped alongside Jagannatha and the Pancha Mahabhuta lingas. The present structure, built in the Kalinga style, stands on foundations far older — inscriptions reference the temple as early as the 7th century CE. Jajpur was the ancient capital of the Keshari dynasty and remains Odisha's most important Shakti centre.
temples.shortDescription: One of the 51 Shakti Peethas where Sati's navel fell — the supreme Shakti seat of Odisha in Kalinga architectural style.

temples.name: Bajreshwari Mata Temple, Kangra
temples.description: The Bajreshwari Mata Temple in Kangra is one of the 51 Shakti Peethas, where the left breast of Sati fell. Vajreshwari — Goddess of the Thunderbolt — is worshipped in her fierce Durga form and serves as the Kuldevi (clan goddess) of Kangra families for generations. The original temple was destroyed by Mahmud of Ghazni and later Mughal rulers, but each time rebuilt, attesting to its extraordinary religious importance. Set in the Kangra Valley with the Dhauladhar mountains as backdrop, the site holds spiritual and cultural significance across Himachal Pradesh.
temples.shortDescription: Kangra's most sacred Shakti Peetha — the left breast of Sati fell here; destroyed by Ghazni and rebuilt, it remains the eternal Kuldevi of Kangra.

temples.name: Ajanta Caves
temples.description: The Ajanta Caves are 30 rock-cut Buddhist cave monuments dating from the second century BCE to about 480 CE in Aurangabad district of Maharashtra state in India. Ajanta Caves are a  UNESCO World Heritage Site. Universally regarded as masterpieces of Buddhist religious art, the caves include paintings and rock-cut sculptures, described as among the finest surviving examples of ancient Indian art, particularly expressive paintings that present emotions through gesture, pose and form.

temples.name: Hemis Monastery and Temple
temples.description: Hemis Monastery, 45 km southeast of Leh in Ladakh, is the largest and wealthiest monastery in the region, belonging to the Drukpa (Red Hat) lineage of Tibetan Buddhism. Though originally founded in the 11th century, it was re-established in 1672 under King Sengge Namgyal. The monastery houses a vast collection of thangkas, stupas, statues, and ancient scriptures. The Hemis Festival, held in June on Guru Padmasambhava's birthday, is Ladakh's most famous celebration—featuring two-day masked cham dances (Tsechu) performed in the open courtyard.
temples.shortDescription: Largest monastery in Ladakh, 45km from Leh. Drukpa lineage, re-established 1672. Famous for the Hemis Festival in June featuring elaborate masked cham dances.

temples.name: Chamunda Devi Temple
temples.description:
Shri Chamunda Devi Temple also known as Chamunda Nandikeshwar Dham is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shri Chamunda Devi, a form of Goddess Durga, located at 19 km away from Palampur town in Dharamshala Tehsil of Kangra district of the Northern Indian state of Himachal Pradesh. This is one of the most prominent temples in Himachal Pradesh and one of the most popular all over India. It is believed that whatever vow is prayed here manifests in reality. 
The much older Aadi Himani Chamunda which is also the original shrine, is situated at the hilltop, making it difficult for pilgrims to reach.

temples.name: Maa Chintpurni Temple
temples.description: Maa Chintpurni Temple at 977 metres in the Shivalik hills of Himachal Pradesh is one of the 51 Shakti Peethas, where Sati's feet fell. The goddess Chhinnamastika — "she who fulfils all wishes and cuts away sorrow" — is worshipped without a formal idol; only the feet (charan) are enshrined, representing the goddess's groundedness and accessibility. One of the most visited temples in Himachal Pradesh, it draws millions annually. The Hindu genealogy registers (vanshavalies) of many Punjabi and Himachali families are maintained here.
temples.shortDescription: Himachal Shakti Peetha where Sati's feet fell — Chhinnamastika, the wish-fulfilling goddess; genealogy registers of Punjabi families kept here.

temples.name: Chamundi Hill Temple
temples.description: Crowning the 3,300-foot Chamundi Hills above Mysuru, the Chamundeshwari Temple enshrines the fierce mother goddess who slew the buffalo demon Mahishasura on this very summit — an act that gave the city its name (Mahishooru → Mysuru). The original shrine is attributed to the 12th-century Hoysala dynasty; the seven-tiered gopuram was added under the Vijayanagara Empire, and a thousand-step staircase was laid in 1659. One of the 18 Shakti Pithas and known as Krouncha Pitha, the temple is deeply woven into the identity of the Mysuru royal family. The Mysore Dasara, Karnataka's state festival, begins and ends here in a pageant of gold, elephants, and devotion.
temples.shortDescription: Shakti Pitha atop Chamundi Hills where the goddess slew Mahishasura — the spiritual heart of the grand Mysore Dasara.

temples.name: Nartiang Durga Temple (Jayanti Peetha)
temples.description: Deep in the forested Jaintia Hills of Meghalaya, the Nartiang Durga Temple marks the spot where, according to Shakta tradition, the left thigh of Sati fell as Vishnu's Sudarshana Chakra severed her body. The presiding goddess is Jayanti — the victorious one. Once the royal shrine of the Jaintia kingdom, the temple complex includes a monolithic monolith field (Nartiang Monoliths) erected by Jaintia kings as memorial megaliths, creating an atmosphere unlike any other Shakti Peetha. The temple's remote northeastern setting and tribal-Hindu syncretism make it one of the more mystical of the 51 sacred seats.
temples.shortDescription: A Shakti Peetha in the misty Jaintia Hills of Meghalaya where Sati's left thigh fell — royal shrine of the ancient Jaintia kingdom.

temples.name: Chintamani Temple
temples.description:
The Chintamani Temple of Theur is a Hindu temple dedicated to Supreme God Ganesha according to Ganapatya Sect located 25 km (16 mi) from Pune, the temple is "one of the larger and more famous" of the Ashtavinayaka, the eight revered shrines of Ganesha in the Indian state of Maharashtra.
The temple lore describes how bhagwan Ganesh retrieved the wish-giving jewel Chintamani for his devotee, the sage Kapila from the greedy king Gana and how he pacifies the uneasy mind of the god Brahma, who meditated on him in Theur.

temples.name: Gangotri Temple
temples.description: Gangotri is a town and a Nagar Panchayat (municipality) in Uttarkashi district in the state of Uttarakhand, India. It is 99 km from Uttarkashi, the main district headquarter. It is a Hindu pilgrim town on the banks of the river Bhagirathi – the origin of the river Ganges. The town is located on the Greater Himalayan Range, at a height of 3,100 metres (10,200 ft). According to a popular Hindu legend, the goddess Ganga descended here when Shiva released the mighty river from the locks of his hair.

temples.name: Guhyeshwari Temple
temples.description: On the sacred southern bank of the Bagmati River, Guhyeshwari enshrines Parvati in her most esoteric form — Guhyeshwari, 'the secret goddess.' Revered as one of the 51 Shakti Peethas, tradition holds that the knees of Sati fell here. Built in the Lichhavi era and renovated by King Pratap Malla in 1653, the temple is a centre of Tantric worship; its inner sanctum is restricted to Hindus, deepening its air of mystery. The Devi Bhagavata Purana venerates this site as Guhyakali, Nepal's own face of the divine feminine.
temples.shortDescription: A Tantric Shakti Peetha on the Bagmati River where Parvati is worshipped in her secret form, Guhyeshwari — believed to mark where Sati's knees fell.

Located in Ayodhya, it is one of the most important temples in the city along with other temples such as Ram Mandir and Nageshwar Nath. This shrine is under the charge of Bairagi Mahants of Ramanandi Sampradaya and Nirvani Akhara.

temples.name: Anjeyanadri Hanuman Temple
temples.description: Anjeyanadri Hill (Anjaneyadri Betta) in Hanumanahalli near Hampi, Karnataka, is revered as the birthplace of the Hindu deity Hanuman, who was born here to Anjana and is thus called Anjaneya — 'son of Anjana'. The hilltop Hanuman temple, reached by approximately 575 steps, houses a rock-carved idol of Lord Hanuman along with shrines of Rama, Sita, and Anjana in the surrounding area. The hill stands within the ancient Kishkinda region of the Ramayana and overlooks the spectacular Hampi landscape and the Tungabhadra River below.

The temple was established by the Hindu preacher and poet-saint Tulsidas in the early 16th century. It is situated on the banks of the Assi river.

temples.name: Yantrodharaka Hanuman Temple
temples.description: The Yantrodharaka Hanuman Temple (also known as Pranadeva Temple) stands atop Malyavana Hill on the banks of the Tungabhadra River in Hampi, Karnataka, and is traditionally identified as the spot where Rama and Hanuman first met. The shrine was eulogised by Vyasaraya, the great 15th-century Madhva saint and poet, who installed the yantra (sacred diagram) beneath the idol — giving the temple its name, 'Yantrodharaka' meaning 'Bearer of the Yantra'. A Kodandarama Temple stands adjacent, and the hilltop location offers sweeping views over the UNESCO World Heritage landscape of Hampi.
temples.shortDescription: 15th-century hilltop Hanuman temple at the legendary spot where Rama and Hanuman first met, eulogised by the Madhva saint Vyasaraya.

temples.name: Hanuman Tok Temple
temples.description: Hanuman Tok perches at 7,200 feet on a forested hill 11 km from central Gangtok, offering panoramic views of the Kanchenjunga range. The site is managed by the Indian Army, and tradition holds that Hanuman rested here during his search for the Sanjeevani herb to revive Lakshmana. The current temple, built by the Indian Army in 1952, draws both Hindu pilgrims and tourists seeking the mountain vistas. It is the highest of Gangtok's accessible temple sites and one of the most scenic shrines in Sikkim.
temples.shortDescription: Indian Army-managed hilltop temple at 7,200 ft near Gangtok, Sikkim. Legend holds Hanuman rested here during his Himalayan journey; panoramic views of Kanchenjunga.

temples.name: Mahavir Mandir
temples.description: Mahavir Mandir is a Hindu temple dedicated to the god Hanuman, located in Patna, Bihar, India. Millions of pilgrims visit the temple every year. Acharya Kishore Kunal is the secretary of the Mahavir Mandir Temple Trust, Patna. After the Vaishno Devi Temple, this temple has the highest earnings of over 1 lakh per day rendering its trust budgets as the second highest in the northern part of the country. The temple attracts huge crowds on festivals like Ramnavami. It is one of the leading temples dedicated to the worship of Lord Hanuman.

temples.name: Mehandipur Balaji Temple
temples.description:
Mehandipur Balaji Temple  is a Hindu temple located in Mehandipur, Dausa district, in the Indian state of Rajasthan. The temple is dedicated to the deity Hanuman. The name Balaji refers to the childhood form of Hanuman, revered in several parts of the country. Unlike similar religious sites, it is located in a town rather than the countryside. Its reputation for ritualistic healing and exorcism of evil spirits attracts many pilgrims from Rajasthan and elsewhere.
The temple is famous worldwide.

temples.name: Hinglaj Mata Temple
temples.description: Deep in the Hingol National Park of Balochistan, Hinglaj Mata marks where the crown of Sati's head fell to earth—among the most sacred of the 51 Shakti Peethas. The goddess is enshrined in a natural mountain cavern on the banks of the Hingol River with no man-made idol, only a sacred rock formation draped in offerings. The Hinglaj Yatra, held each April over four days, draws an estimated 800,000 Hindu and Zikri Muslim pilgrims, making it the largest annual Hindu pilgrimage in Pakistan. The Pakistani military and government provide security and logistics for the yatra, reflecting the shrine's trans-religious significance.
temples.shortDescription: Shakti Peetha in Balochistan, Pakistan, where Sati's crown fell. A natural cave shrine drawing ~800,000 pilgrims each April—the largest Hindu pilgrimage in Pakistan.

temples.description: Suryanar Kovil (also called Suryanar Temple) is a Hindu temple dedicated to the deity Hindu Sun-God, Surya, located in near a small town Aduthurai, Thanjavur District in Tamil Nadu, India. The presiding deity is Suriyanar, the Sun and his consorts Ushadevi and Pratyusha Devi. The temple also has separate shrines for the other eight planetary deities. The temple is considered one of the nine Navagraha temples in Tamil Nadu. The temple is one of the few historic temples dedicated to Sun god and is also the only temple in Tamil Nadu which has shrines for all the planetary deities.

temples.name: Jagannath Temple
temples.description: Built in the Kalinga style by Anantavarman Chodaganga of the Eastern Ganga dynasty in the 12th century, the Jagannath Temple in Puri is among India's most revered shrines — and one of the four Char Dham. Lord Jagannath, along with siblings Balabhadra and Subhadra, is enshrined on the jewelled Ratnabedi platform as deities carved from sacred neem wood. Entry is restricted to Hindus. The temple's Rath Yatra — when the three deities ride massive wooden chariots through Puri's Grand Road — is one of the largest annual religious gatherings on earth.
temples.shortDescription: A 12th-century Kalinga-style Char Dham temple in Puri housing Lord Jagannath, famed for the annual Rath Yatra chariot festival — one of the world's largest religious processions.

temples.name: Khandagiri and Udayagiri Caves
temples.description: Khandagiri and Udayagiri are twin hills near Bhubaneswar, Odisha, containing 117 rock-cut cells and caves dating to the 1st–2nd century BCE, commissioned by the Jain king Kharavela of the Mahameghavahana dynasty. Udayagiri (Sunrise Hill) has 18 caves including the elaborate double-story Rani Gumpha (Queen's Cave) with narrative friezes. Khandagiri (Broken Hill) has 15 caves, one still functioning as an active Jain temple. The site is the earliest surviving example of Odisha's rock-cut architecture and the most significant Jain monastic complex in eastern India.
temples.shortDescription: Twin hills near Bhubaneswar with 117 Jain rock-cut caves from the 1st century BCE. Commissioned by King Kharavela; features the elaborate Rani Gumpha with narrative friezes.

temples.name: Parsvanath Jain Temple
temples.description: Rising above the eastern group of Khajuraho, this 10th-century Digambara Jain temple stands as the largest of all the Jain shrines in the complex. Commissioned around 950–970 CE during the reign of Chandela king Dhanga, it was originally consecrated to Adinatha before being rededicated to Parshvanatha. Its outer walls carry a remarkable duality — Jain celestial beings share space with Vaishnavite imagery. A 1011 VS inscription records donations from a merchant named Pahila. The temple harbors one of the oldest known 4×4 most-perfect magic squares, the famed 'Jaina square,' inscribed on its walls — a mathematical wonder woven into sacred stone.
temples.shortDescription: Largest Jain temple at Khajuraho, famed for its dual Hindu-Jain sculptural program and the ancient Jaina magic square inscription.

temples.name: Dilwara Temples
temples.description: The Delwada Temples or Delvada Temples  are a group of Śvētāmbara Jain temples located about 2+1⁄2 kilometres from the Mount Abu settlement in Sirohi District, Rajasthan's only hill station. The earliest were built by Vimal Shah, a Jain minister of Solanki king of Gurjaratra, Bhima I and  additions to the temples were made by Vastupala, Jain minister of Vaghelas of Gurjaratra.  They date between the 11th and 16th centuries, forming some of the most famous monuments in the style of Solanki architecture, famous for their use of a very pure white marble and intricate marble carvings.

temples.name: Jessoreswari Kali Temple
temples.description: Jessoreswari Kali Temple in Ishwaripur, Satkhira district, Bangladesh, is one of the 51 Shakti Peethas—the palms and soles of Sati are said to have fallen here. The presiding deity is Jessoreswari (Yashoreshwari), a form of Kali. The temple gained international attention in 2021 when Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi visited during his Bangladesh state visit, offering worship here and at Orakandi. The site is tended by a small Hindu community and represents the deep historical Hindu presence in the Bengal delta.

temples.name: Jwalamukhi Temple
temples.description: Jwalamukhi Temple in Kangra district is among the most extraordinary of the 51 Shakti Peethas—the goddess manifests not as an idol but as nine eternal blue flames rising from natural gas fissures in the rock, said to be Sati's tongue. Akbar famously tried to extinguish the flames with a channel of diverted water, failed, and offered a golden canopy (chattar) in acknowledgment; the goddess is said to have bent it out of shape in response. A gilded dome crowns the main shrine. The temple is one of the 64 tantric pithas and draws massive pilgrimage, especially during Navratri.
temples.shortDescription: Shakti Peetha in Kangra where nine eternal natural gas flames represent Sati's tongue. Akbar tried and failed to douse the flames, then offered a golden canopy.

temples.name: Kalighat Kali Temple
temples.description:
Kalighat Kali Temple is a Hindu temple in Kalighat, Kolkata, West Bengal, India, dedicated to the Hindu goddess Kali, one of the 10 Mahavidyas in the Hindu tantric tradition and the supreme deity in the Kalikula worship tradition. The temple is one of the 51 Shakti Pithas in India.
According to the Devi Bhagavata Purana, Kalika Purana and Shakti Peetha Stotram, the toes of the right foot of Goddess Sati fell here, after Lord Vishnu's Sudarshan Chakra splintered her body into many parts to calm down Mahadev's rage during his cosmic dance.

temples.name: Dakshineswar Kali Temple
temples.description: Dakshineswar Kali Temple or Dakshineswar Kalibari is a Hindu navaratna style temple in Dakshineswar, Kolkata, West Bengal, India, on the eastern bank of the Hooghly River. The presiding deity of the temple is Bhavatarini (Kali), a form of Mahadevi or Parashakti Adya Kali, otherwise known as Adishakti Kalika. The temple was built in 1855 by Rani Rashmoni, a zamindar (feudal lord), and a devotee of Kali. The temple is associated with avatar Sri Ramakrishna and Sarada Devi, his wife and devotee mystic, both of 19th century Bengal.

temples.description: Bhadrakali Temple (Nepali :भद्रकाली मन्दिर) is a temple on the East of Pokhara in Kundahar, atop a small hill.  It is dedicated to the Goddess Kali. Founded in the year 1817, the temple shrines the Hindu goddess of power and strength. Bhadrakali or Durga covers an area of 135 ropanies. There are two ways to reach atop: the eastern way has 292 steps to reach the temple while traversing from the southern part is only 265 steps. In the year 1817, the temple was previously known as “Mudule Thumpko.

temples.name: Kamakhya Temple
temples.description: Atop the Nilachal Hills overlooking the Brahmaputra, Kamakhya Temple is the supreme tantric shrine of Hinduism—a yoni peetha where the goddess is worshipped in her procreative form, with no anthropomorphic idol. The sanctum holds a cleft rock perpetually moistened by an underground spring, representing Shakti's womb. Rebuilt in the distinctive Nilachal architectural style in 1565 CE after Mughal destruction, it is the heart of the Kulachara Tantra tradition. The Ambubachi Mela each June, celebrating the goddess's annual menstruation, draws 50,000+ tantric sadhus and pilgrims from across India for four days.

temples.name: Kamakshi Amman Temple
temples.description: The Kamakshi Amman Temple in Kanchipuram is one of India's three supreme Shakti Peethas — alongside Madurai Meenakshi and Varanasi Visalakshi. Kamakshi — "she whose eyes are love" — is Adi Parashakti herself, uniquely seated in the Srichakra posture (padmasana), representing conquest of all desires. Adi Shankaracharya is credited with installing the Srichakra yantra to pacify the fierce goddess. The Chola and Vijayanagara gopurams and Pallava-era mandapas make this one of South India's architecturally richest and most spiritually charged complexes.

temples.name: Kankalitala Temple
temples.description: Kankalitala Temple in Bolpur, Birbhum district, West Bengal, marks where Sati's waist (kankala, meaning skeleton) fell. The presiding goddess is Kankaleshwari (Devagarbha) and the Bhairava is Ruru. The temple sits on the banks of the Kopai River near Bolpur, the town most associated with Rabindranath Tagore's Shantiniketan ashram, giving this Shakti Peetha an unusual cultural context. The site is serene and relatively undeveloped, retaining a rural sacred atmosphere.

temples.name: Dwarkadhish Temple
temples.description: The Dwarkadhish temple, also known as the Jagat Mandir and occasionally spelled Dwarakadheesh, is a Hindu temple dedicated to Krishna, who is worshiped in the temple by the name Dwarkadhish (Dvārakādhīśa), or 'King of Dwarka'. The temple is located at Dwarka city of Gujarat, India, which is one of the destinations of Char Dham, a Hindu pilgrimage circuit. The main shrine of the five-storied building, supported by 72 pillars, is known as Jagat Mandir or Nija Mandir. Archaeological findings suggest the original temple was built in 200 BCE at the earliest.

temples.name: Bankey Bihari Temple
temples.description:
Banke Bihari Temple is a Hindu temple situated in the town of Vrindavan, Mathura district of Uttar Pradesh, India. The temple is dedicated to Banke Bihari who is believed to be the combined form of Radha and Krishna. Banke Bihari was originally worshipped at Nidhivan, Vrindavan. Later, when Banke Bihari temple was constructed around 1864, the icon of Banke Bihari was moved to its present temple.
In the Banke Bihari temple, the icon of Radha Krishna's united form stands in the Tribhanga posture.

Shri Radha Vallabh Temple, also called Shri Radha Vallabhlal ji Temple is a historic temple in the city of Vrindavan, Mathura district, Uttar Pradesh, India. The temple is dedicated to Hindu deities Radha Krishna. The temple belongs to Radha Vallabh Sampradaya and was constructed in 16th century under the guidance of Vrindavan saint Hith Harivansha Mahaprabhu.
The central deity of the temple is Krishna who is worshiped under the name of Shri Radha Vallabh which means the consort of Radha. Alongside Krishna, a crown is placed which signifies the presence of goddess Radha.

temples.name: Temple of the Vedic Planetarium
temples.description: Rising 340 feet above the sacred floodplains where the Ganges meets the Jalangi, the Temple of the Vedic Planetarium is unlike any structure attempted in the modern era. Its Capitol-inspired dome shelters a cosmological universe drawn from the Srimad Bhagavatam — a spiralling Vedic cosmos with Mount Meru at its centre. Conceived by Srila Prabhupada in 1976 and built upon land identified as the birthplace of Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, it is under construction since 2009. During Gaura Purnima, hundreds of thousands of devotees from every continent circumambulate in night-long sankirtan.
temples.shortDescription: The world's largest temple under construction — a Vedic cosmological wonder rising 340 ft above Sri Chaitanya's sacred birthplace.

temples.name: ISKCON Temple Pune
temples.description: Nestled in the hills of Kondhwa on Pune's southern edge, the New Vedic Cultural Center is far more than a place of worship. Three golden altars blaze with the gold-plated forms of Sri Sri Radha Vrindavanchandra, Sri Sri Gaura-Nitai, and Sri Sri Jagannath-Baldev-Subhadra, their brilliance amplified by cascading chandeliers and ceiling murals depicting Krishna's eternal pastimes. Vedic aesthetics drawn from Jagannath Puri and Tirupati converge here, inspiring awe and inner stillness. The ceaseless sound of kirtana fills the marble halls, drawing thousands of seekers each day.
temples.shortDescription: Pune's grandest ISKCON temple — three gold-plated altars, Vedic murals and ceaseless kirtan draw thousands of Krishna devotees daily.

temples.name: Mangalagauri Temple
temples.description: Perched atop Mangalagauri Hill in the ancient pilgrimage city of Gaya, this Maha Shakti Peetha marks where the breast of Goddess Sati fell — a symbol of divine nourishment and cosmic motherhood. The hillside sanctum is deliberately intimate; only two to five worshippers may enter at a time, creating an atmosphere of intense personal devotion. An Akhand Deep burns ceaselessly within, and subsidiary shrines to Kali, Ganesha, Hanuman and Shiva surround the main goddess.

The peetha is located in the sacred town of Pushkar, already renowned for the only Brahma temple in India and the Pushkar Lake, making this one of the most ritually layered pilgrimage towns in Hinduism. Pilgrims visiting Pushkar Lake and the Brahma temple commonly also pay homage at Manibandh. The Pushkar Mela in November adds another dimension to the site's spiritual calendar.

Maya Devi Temple, Haridwar (Hindi: माया देवी मंदिर, हरिद्वार) is a Hindu temple dedicated to goddess Maya in the holy city of Haridwar of Uttarakhand state in India. It is believed that the heart and navel of goddess Sati fell in the region where the temple stands today and thus it is sometimes referred to as a Shakta pitha.
Goddess Maya is the Adhisthatri deity of Haridwar. She is a three-headed and four-armed deity who is believed to be an incarnation of Shakti. Haridwar was previously known as Mayapuri in reverence to this deity.

temples.description: Naina Devi Temple crowns a dramatic hilltop in Bilaspur district, Himachal Pradesh, with sweeping views over the Gobind Sagar reservoir, and marks where Sati's right eye (naina) fell to earth. A ropeway connects the base town to the hilltop shrine, making the pilgrimage accessible to all. The main idol shows Naina Devi flanked by Ganesh and Hanuman, with a sacred footprint of Guru Gobind Singh also venerated here. The temple is managed by the Naina Devi Shrine Board and Navratri draws up to half a million pilgrims over nine days—one of Himachal Pradesh's largest religious gatherings.

temples.name: Parashurama Temple
temples.description: Parashurama Temple in Chiplun, Ratnagiri district, is the most important shrine to Parashurama—the sixth avatar of Vishnu—in the Konkan coast region he is said to have created by hurling his axe into the sea to reclaim land. The present temple was built around 1700 CE, 11 km from Chiplun town, on a site long associated with Parashurama's penance after the Kshatriya wars. The Konkan coast (Parashurama Kshetra) is considered Parashurama's domain, and this temple is the primary pilgrimage destination for his devotees in Maharashtra.

temples.name: Patan Devi Temple
temples.description: Patan Devi is credited with giving Patna its very name, and stands as one of the city's oldest shrines. Two temples — Badi (Big) and Chhoti (Small) Patan Devi — form a dual Shakti Peetha complex where the right thigh of Goddess Sati is believed to have fallen. Images were reportedly installed by Man Singh, general of Emperor Akbar, lending the site a rare confluence of Brahmanical and Mughal heritage. Each morning and evening priests bathe the goddess, offer prasad and perform aarti to ringing bells.

temples.description: Prem Mandir ("Temple of Divine Love") in Vrindavan is one of India's most spectacular modern temples, established by Jagadguru Kripalu Ji Maharaj. Built over 11 years in pure white Rajasthani marble, the complex depicts the divine love of Radha-Krishna and Sita-Rama through exquisite carvings on every surface. The evening illumination — when the temple glows in changing coloured lights — draws thousands nightly. The 54-acre campus includes gardens recreating Krishna's Vrindavan lilas and life-size dioramas of key episodes from the Ramayana and Bhagavatam.
temples.shortDescription: Vrindavan's most spectacular modern marble temple — Prem Mandir glows nightly in coloured lights, celebrating Radha-Krishna and Sita-Rama divine love.

temples.name: ISKCON Temple Vrindavan
temples.description: In the sacred dust of Vrindavan — where Krishna himself once played — stands Srila Prabhupada's masterwork: a gleaming white marble temple inaugurated on Ram Navami 1975 and now one of the holiest Vaishnava destinations in the world. Three altars house Krishna-Balaram, Radha-Shyamasundar with the gopis, and Gaura-Nitai. Pilgrims descend into a sunken courtyard shaded by an ancient tamal tree — believed to be the very species Krishna would rest beneath — and experience a timeless intimacy with the Divine that the chaos of modern Vrindavan cannot penetrate.

temples.name: Ram Janmabhoomi Temple
temples.description: Ram Mandir stands at the heart of one of India's most consequential religious disputes — the Ram Janmabhoomi conflict — resolved by the Supreme Court in 2019. Designed in the Nagara style by architect Chandrakant Sompura, the three-storeyed pink sandstone structure soars 161 feet and was consecrated on 22 January 2024 in a ceremony presided over by the Prime Minister. The presiding deity is Ram Lalla — the infant form of Rama — installed at his mythological birthplace.
temples.shortDescription: A monumental Nagara-style temple consecrated in January 2024, built on the site revered as the birthplace of Lord Ram after a landmark Supreme Court verdict.

temples.name: Chitrakoot Dham
temples.description: Chitrakoot is among India's most sacred pilgrimage centres — Lord Ram, Sita and Lakshmana spent the first 11.5 years of their 14-year exile here in the Vindhya forest. The sacred Kamadgiri hill (circumambulated 5km barefoot) is believed to be Ram's forest abode and is so sacred that no photography is permitted. The Mandakini River, Ramghat, Sphatik Shila and countless ashrams make this a living Ramayana landscape. Saints including Tulsidas completed the Ramcharitmanas here; Adi Shankaracharya and Vivekananda both meditated at Chitrakoot.
temples.shortDescription: Ram, Sita and Lakshmana's 11.5-year forest home — Kamadgiri hill circumambulation, Mandakini ghats and the living Ramayana landscape of the Vindhyas.

temples.name: Bharat Mandir, Nandigram
temples.description: Nandigram, known by its ancient name Bharatkund, is a sacred village in the Sohawal tehsil of Ayodhya district, Uttar Pradesh, approximately 19 km south of Ayodhya city. It is revered in the Ramayana tradition as the place where Prince Bharata, refusing to rule from the capital in his brother Rama's absence, placed Rama's padukas (sandals) on the throne and administered the kingdom in symbolic regency from this village. The Bharat Mandir and the sacred Bharat Sarovar (kund) here draw pilgrims from across India who honour Bharata's legendary devotion and renunciation.
temples.shortDescription: Sacred village where Prince Bharata governed during Rama's exile, enshrining Rama's padukas on the throne as an act of unwavering devotion.

temples.name: Kanak Bhawan
temples.description: Kanak Bhawan (lit. 'Golden Palace') is a temple in Ram Janmabhoomi, Ayodhya, which is in the north-east of Ramkot. It is one of the most famous temples of Ayodhya. It is believed that this palace (temple) was gifted by Kaikeyi, immediately after the marriage of Rama with Sita and hence, it is their personal palace. According to the mythology, after the original Kanak Bhawan was damaged, it was rebuilt by Krishna himself in the Dvapar Yuga. It is believed that it was renovated by Vikramaditya in the medieval period.

temples.description: Revered as Dakshina Ayodhya, Bhadrachalam is where the saint-poet Kancherla Gopanna diverted Golconda Sultanate treasury funds to build this temple — and spent 12 years in prison for it, composing devotional songs to Rama that are still sung here today. temples.name: Rukmini Devi Temple
temples.description:
The Rukmini Devi Temple is a temple in Dwarka, 2 kilometres (1.2 mi) away from Dwarka, Gujarat, India. It is dedicated to Goddess Rukmini (Lord Krishna's chief queen, beloved consort and the incarnation of Devi Mahalakshmi in Dvapara Yuga).  The temple is believed to date back to the 5th century BC, though its present form is thought to originate from the 12th century.
It is a richly carved temple decorated with sculptures of gods and goddesses on the exterior with the sanctum housing the main image of Rukmini.

temples.name: Shirdi Sai Baba Temple
temples.description: The Shri Saibaba Samadhi Mandir is built over the sacred resting place of Sai Baba of Shirdi, a saint venerated by Hindus, Muslims and Zoroastrians alike. Originally the Buti Wada — a stone mansion built by Nagpur millionaire Gopalrao Buti between 1915 and 1918 — it became Sai Baba's samadhi on 15 October 1918, his Mahasamadhi day. The marble shrine now draws an estimated 60,000 pilgrims daily, rising to over 200,000 during major festivals.
temples.shortDescription: Built over Sai Baba's samadhi, Shirdi's marble-and-gold shrine draws 60,000 pilgrims daily and is one of India's most visited pilgrimage sites.

temples.name: Saptashrungi Temple
temples.description: Saptashrungi Devi is enshrined on one of seven peaks (sapta shrungi) in the Sahyadri ranges of Nashik district, Maharashtra, revered as one of Maharashtra's 3.5 Shakti Pithas—the 'half-pitha' in the traditional reckoning. The swayambhu (self-manifested) image, carved into an 8-metre cliff face, depicts the eighteen-armed goddess holding weapons in each hand—no human artist carved this icon, it emerged from the living rock. The climb of 472 steps through forested hillside is itself a devotional act. Chaitrotsav in March/April and Navratri in October are the two great festivals.
temples.shortDescription: One of Maharashtra's 3.5 Shakti Pithas near Nashik. An 18-armed swayambhu goddess image carved into a cliff face, reached by 472 steps through the Sahyadri hills.

temples.name: Savitri Mata Mandir
temples.description: Savitri Mata Mandir crowns the Ratnagiri Hill above Pushkar at 750 feet, reached by a steep climb of 970 steps or a ropeway. The goddess Savitri — Brahma's first wife — sits here in dignity overlooking the sacred lake and the sole Brahma Temple below. Local legend says she cursed Brahma to be worshipped nowhere but Pushkar after he replaced her with Gayatri at a yagna. The hilltop view over Pushkar's ghats and sand dunes at sunrise, with bells rising from below, is among Rajasthan's most atmospheric pilgrimage experiences.
temples.shortDescription: Hilltop temple to Savitri, Brahma's first consort, overlooking Pushkar Lake — 970 steps to a summit with sweeping Rajasthan desert views.

temples.name: Vaishno Devi Temple
temples.description: Vaishno Devi Temple, also known as the Shri Mata Vaishno Devi Temple and Vaishno Devi Bhavan, is a Hindu temple in Katra, Reasi district, Jammu and Kashmir, India. Dedicated to Vaishno Devi, a manifestation of godesses Mahakali, Mahalakshmi, and Mahasarasvati, it is on Trikuta mountain at an elevation of 5,200 feet (1,500 meters). The Shakti tradition considers it to be a Shakti Pitha. The temple is 43 km from the main city of Jammu and 29 km from the district headquarters Reasi town.

temples.name: Kanchi Kamakoti Peetham
temples.description: Nestled in the ancient temple city of Kanchipuram, the Sri Kanchi Kamakoti Peetham is one of Hinduism's most venerable Advaita Vedanta institutions, traditionally ascribed to Adi Shankaracharya. For centuries it has been a living centre of Vedic learning and Sanskrit scholarship, its spiritual identity woven intimately with the goddess Kamakshi. The Peetham rose to global prominence under its 68th Shankaracharya, Sri Chandrashekharendra Saraswati, who is widely revered as a saint.
temples.shortDescription: One of Hinduism's most venerable Advaita Vedanta institutions in Kanchipuram, traditionally founded by Adi Shankaracharya and inseparable from the goddess Kamakshi.

temples.name: Patan Rani ki Vav
temples.description: Conceived as an inverted temple to water, Rani ki Vav descends seven breathtaking levels into the earth along the banks of the Saraswati River in Patan, Gujarat. Queen Udayamati commissioned it in the 11th century as an act of devotion to her late husband, King Bhimdev I of the Solanki dynasty. Every surface is alive with artistry: over 500 principal sculptures and a thousand minor carvings portray the full Hindu pantheon — Vishnu in his ten avatars, Shiva, Parvati, Saraswati — rendered in the finest Maru-Gurjara tradition. Buried under silt for centuries and reborn through excavation, it earned UNESCO World Heritage status in 2014 and graces the Indian ₹100 note.
temples.shortDescription: 11th-century UNESCO stepwell in Patan, Gujarat — an inverted temple of 500+ sculptures built by Queen Udayamati.

Known in particular for its library, stories recount scholars travelling long distances to access its texts.

temples.name: Maa Sharda Mandir, Maihar
temples.description: Maa Sharda Mandir at Maihar crowns Trikoota Hill with 1,063 steps above the Vindhya plains. The goddess Sharda — worshipped as an incarnation of Saraswati — is also revered as a Shakti Peetha. Legend holds that the immortal warrior Alha still climbs these steps to worship at midnight, devoted to the goddess centuries after the Battle of Mahoba. Ustad Allauddin Khan, the sitar maestro who shaped Indian classical music, was Sharda's devoted son. The hilltop sunrise, with temple bells ringing over the Vindhyas, is among central India's most moving pilgrimage moments.
temples.shortDescription: Vindhya hilltop Sharda temple with 1,063 steps — Saraswati incarnate, sacred to Alha the immortal warrior and sitar maestro Allauddin Khan.

temples.name: Sheetla Mata Temple, Gurugram
temples.description: The Sheetla Mata Temple in Gurugram is one of the 51 Shakti Peethas and the Kuldevi (clan goddess) of the region. Sheetla — the "cool goddess" — protects against smallpox, fever and skin diseases; her idol is draped in blue and she rides a donkey carrying a broom to sweep away disease. The annual Sheetla Ashtami, held on the eighth day after Holi, is one of Haryana's largest religious fairs. It is the only Indian festival where leftover cold food (basi khana) is the sacred prasad — offered cold to honour the goddess of coolness.
temples.shortDescription: Gurugram Shakti Peetha and Kuldevi — the "cool goddess" who wards off disease; celebrated by offering cold leftover food on Sheetla Ashtami.

temples.name: Kashi Vishwanath Temple
temples.description:
Kashi Vishwanath Temple is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva. It is located in Vishwanath Gali, in Varanasi, Uttar Pradesh, India. The temple is a Hindu pilgrimage site and is one of the twelve Jyotirlinga shrines. The presiding deity is known by the names Vishwanath and Vishweshwara (IAST: Viśvanātha and Viśveśvara), meaning Lord of the Universe.
The original temple, called the Adi Vishveshwar Temple, was demolished by Mohammad of Ghor during his invasion of India. Subsequently, the template was rebuilt by Man Singh I and Todar Mal under the emperor Akbar.

temples.name: Kedarnath Temple
temples.description:
Kēdāranātha Temple (Sanskrit: केदारनाथ मंदिर, IAST: Kēdāranātha Mandira, lit. 'temple of the God of the field') is a Hindu temple, one of the twelve jyotirlinga of Śiva. The temple is located on the Garhwal Himalayan range 
near the Mandākinī river, in the state of Uttarakhand, India. Due to extreme weather conditions, the temple is open to the general public only between the months of April (Akṣaya Tritiya) and November (Kārtika Pūrṇimā, the autumn full moon). During the winters, the vigraha (deity) of the temple is carried down to Ukhimath to be worshiped for the next six months.

temples.name: Kailasa Temple
temples.description:
The Kailasha (IAST: Kailāśa) or Kailasha-natha (IAST: Kailāśa-nātha) temple is the largest of the rock-cut Hindu temples at the Ellora Caves in Sambhaji Nagar district of Maharashtra, India.
A megalith carved into a cliff face, it is considered one of the most remarkable cave temples in the world because of its size, architecture, and sculptural treatment. It has been called "the climax of the rock-cut phase of Indian architecture". The top of the structure over the sanctuary is 32.6 metres (107 ft) above the level of the court below.

temples.name: Elephanta Caves
temples.description: The Elephanta Caves form a collection of cave temples predominantly dedicated to the Hindu god Shiva; UNESCO has designated them as a World Heritage Site. They are located on Elephanta Island, or Gharapuri (literally meaning "the city of the caves"), in Mumbai Harbour, 10 kilometres (6.2 mi) east of Mumbai in the Indian state of Maharashtra. The island, about 2 kilometres (1.2 mi) west of the Jawaharlal Nehru Port, features five Hindu caves, a few Buddhist stupa mounds that date back to the 2nd century BCE, and two Buddhist caves with water tanks.

temples.description:
The Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram is a collection of 7th- and 8th-century CE religious monuments in the coastal resort town of Mahabalipuram, Tamil Nadu, India and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It is on the Coromandel Coast of the Bay of Bengal, about 60 kilometres (37 mi) south of Chennai.
The site has 40 ancient monuments and Hindu temples, including one of the largest open-air rock reliefs in the world: the Descent of the Ganges or Arjuna's Penance.

temples.name: Lingaraja Temple
temples.description:
Lingaraja Temple (Odia: [liŋɡɔraːd͡ʒɔ] ) is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva and is one of the oldest temples in Bhubaneswar, the capital of the Indian state of Odisha, India. The temple is the most prominent landmark of Bhubaneswar city and one of the major tourist attractions of the state. Shiva's consort and the temple's presiding Goddess, Parvati, is referred to as Annapurna or Girija.
The Lingaraja temple is the largest temple in Bhubaneswar. The central tower of the temple is 180 ft (55 m) tall.

temples.description: Hoysaleshwara temple (also spelled as Hoysaleswara, Hoysaleśvara or Hoywalesvara temple), also referred simply as the Halebidu temple, is a 12th-century Hindu temple dedicated to the god Shiva. It is the largest monument in Halebidu, a town in the state of Karnataka, India, and the former capital of the Hoysala Empire. The temple was built on the banks of a large man-made lake, and sponsored by King Vishnuvardhana of the Hoysala Empire. Its construction started around 1121 CE and was complete in 1160 CE.

temples.name: Gangaikonda Cholapuram Temple
temples.description: The Brihadisvara Temple is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva in Gangaikonda Cholapuram, Jayankondam, in the South Indian state of Tamil Nadu. Completed in 1035 CE by Rajendra Chola I as a part of his new capital, this Chola dynasty era temple is similar in design, and has a similar name, as the older 11th century, Brihadeeswarar Temple about 70 kilometres (43 mi) to the southwest in Thanjavur. The Gangaikonda Cholapuram Temple is smaller yet more refined than the Thanjavur Temple. Both are among the largest Shiva temples in South India and examples of Dravidian style temples.

temples.name: Airavatesvara Temple
temples.description:
Airavatesvara Temple is a Hindu temple of Chola architecture located in Darasuram, a suburb of Kumbakonam, Thanjavur District in the South Indian state of Tamil Nadu. This temple, built by Chola emperor Rajaraja II in the 12th century CE is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, along with the Brihadeeswara Temple at Thanjavur, the Gangaikondacholisvaram Temple at Gangaikonda Cholapuram that are referred to as the Great Living Chola Temples.
The Airavatesvarar temple is one among a cluster of eighteen medieval era large Hindu temples in the Kumbakonam area, Thanjavur District.

temples.name: Thiruvaiyaru Temple
temples.description: On the banks of the Kaveri in Thiruvaiyaru, the Aiyarappar Temple venerates Shiva as the lord presiding over five sacred rivers: Vadavaru, Vennaaru, Vettaaru, Kudamuruttiyaaru, and Kaveri. The name itself encodes this identity — ai meaning five, aru meaning river in Tamil. Revered in the 7th-century Tevaram hymns of the Nayanar saint-poets and classified as a Paadal Petra Sthalam, this is known as Dakshina Kailasam — the Kailasa of the South. Inscriptions from the Chola, Pandya, Nayak, and Maratha eras attest to centuries of royal devotion. The temple is equally celebrated as the site of the annual Thyagaraja Aradhana, drawing the finest Carnatic musicians each January.
temples.shortDescription: Dakshina Kailasam on the Kaveri, where Shiva lords over five rivers and Carnatic music fills the air each January.

temples.name: Thiruporur Temple
temples.description: Situated 28 km from Chennai, the Thiruporur Kandaswamy Temple is one of the most revered shrines of Murugan in Tamil Nadu. The presiding deity Kandaswamy — a seven-foot standing granite form of Murugan bearing the Vel (divine spear) — is believed to bestow victory and protection. Legend holds that Murugan halted here after his cosmic victory over the demon Surapadman. The five-tiered rajagopuram soaring 70 feet anchors a four-acre complex where Valli and Deivaanai are enshrined. It draws enormous devotional crowds, especially during Kavadi-carrying festivals that turn the approach road into a river of faith.
temples.shortDescription: Ancient Murugan shrine 28 km from Chennai, famous for Kavadi festivals and the towering image of Kandaswamy bearing his Vel.

temples.name: Kailasanathar Temple
temples.description: The Kailasanathar Temple, Kanchipuram, also referred to as the Kailasanatha temple, is a Pallava-era historic Hindu temple in Kanchipuram, Tamil Nadu, India. Dedicated to Shiva, it is one of the oldest surviving monuments in Kanchipuram. It reflects a Dravidian architecture and was built around 700 CE by Narasimhavarman II with additions by Mahendravarman III. A square-plan temple, it has a mukha-mandapa (entrance hall), a maha-mandapa (gathering hall) and a primary garbha-griya (sanctum) topped with a four-storey vimana.

temples.name: Kedareshwara Temple
temples.description: Kedareshwara Temple (also spelt "Kedaresvara" or "Kedareshvara") is a Hoysala era construction in the historically important town of Halebidu, in the Hassan district of Karnataka state, India. It is located a short distance away from the famous Hoysaleswara Temple. The temple was constructed by Hoysala King Veera Ballala II (r. 1173–1220 CE) and his Queen Ketaladevi, and the main deity is Ishwara (another name for the Hindu god Shiva). The temple is protected as a monument of national importance by the Archaeological Survey of India.

temples.name: Kandariya Mahadev Temple
temples.description: The Kandariya Mahadeva Temple (Devanagari: कंदारिया महादेव मंदिर, Kandāriyā Mahādeva Mandir), meaning "the Great God of the Cave", is the largest and most ornate Hindu temple in the medieval temple group found at Khajuraho in Madhya Pradesh, India. It is considered as one of the best examples of temples preserved from the medieval period in India. Because of its outstanding preservation and testimony to the Chandela culture, the temple was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1986.

It is dedicated to Vaikuntha Vishnu - an aspect of Vishnu. As part of the Khajuraho Group of Monuments, and owing to its architecture and religious importance, the temple was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1986.

temples.name: Vishvanath Temple
temples.description: Commissioned by Chandela king Dhanga and completed around 999–1002 CE, the Vishvanatha Temple dedicated to Shiva — 'Lord of the Universe' — represents the apex of the Khajuraho stylistic arc. Conceived as a panchayatana complex with a principal shrine flanked by subsidiary shrines, a magnificent 2.2-metre Nandi faces the sanctum from the east. The sculptural program celebrates Shiva's cosmos — surasundaris, amorous couples, and celestial beings cover three bands in a vision of divine abundance that has drawn pilgrims for over a millennium.
temples.shortDescription: Chandela Shiva masterpiece at Khajuraho — near-perfect panchayatana layout with a 2.2 m Nandi and renowned sculptural friezes.

temples.name: Devi Jagadambi Temple
temples.description:
Devi Jagadambika Temple or Jagadambika Temple is one of a group of about 25 temples at Khajuraho, Madhya Pradesh, India. Along with the other temples at Khajuraho, the temple was listed as a World Heritage Site because of its outstanding architecture, art, and historical importance. The temples of Khajuraho were built by the rulers of the Chandela dynasty between the 10th and the 12th centuries.
Devi Jagadambika temple, in a group to the north, is one of the most finely decorated temples at Khajuraho. It is named after Jagadambika, a Hindu goddess related to devi.

temples.name: Siddhanath Temple, Nemawar
temples.description: The Siddhanath Temple at Nemawar stands on the sacred banks of the Narmada River, built in the 10th century under the Paramara dynasty from exquisitely carved yellow and blush sandstone. A masterpiece of Nagara architecture, its mandapa, antechamber and sanctum form a perfectly proportioned ensemble above the flowing river. The presiding deity Siddhanath — a form of Shiva — is accompanied by sculptures of exceptional quality. The riverside setting on India's holiest river amplifies both the temple's sanctity and its visual drama.
temples.shortDescription: 10th-century Paramara-dynasty Shiva temple of yellow sandstone on the banks of the Narmada — a Nagara architectural gem of Madhya Pradesh.

temples.name: Annamalaiyar Temple
temples.description: At the foot of the ancient red hill of Arunachala — believed by devotees to be Shiva himself made manifest on earth — this temple enshrines the Agni Lingam, the fire element among the Pancha Bhuta Sthalas. Legend holds that Shiva appeared here as an endless column of light, settling an eternal dispute between Brahma and Vishnu. Each year, millions converge for Karthigai Deepam, when a great beacon of ghee and camphor is lit atop the hill at the precise moment the full moon rises, making the entire sky glow. Sages through the ages, most famously Ramana Maharshi, have declared Arunachala not merely a sacred site but Shiva's living form — a hill that is itself the guru.
temples.shortDescription: Pancha Bhuta Sthala enshrining Shiva as the Agni Lingam at the sacred Arunachala hill, Tiruvannamalai.

temples.name: Mundeshwari Temple
temples.description:
The Mundeshwari Devi Temple (IAST: Muṇḍeśvarī) is a Hindu temple, located at Ramgarh Village, 608 feet (185 m) on the Mundeshwari Hills of Kaimur plateau near Son River, in the Bhojpuri region of Indian state of Bihar. It is an Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) protected monument since 1915.
It is an ancient temple which is believed to be dedicated to the worship of the goddess Durga and god Shiva, and is claimed as the oldest functional Hindu temple in the world.

temples.name: Amarnath Cave Temple
temples.description: At 3,888 m in the Himalayas, Amarnath is a cave shrine where meltwater dripping from a 40-metre limestone roof freezes into a natural ice stalagmite worshipped as the Swayambhu Shiva lingam. The lingam waxes through summer as Himalayan snow melts, then recedes — nearby formations are identified as Parvati and Ganesha. Mentioned in the 6th-century Nilamata Purana, the cave is accessible only during the Shravani Mela (late June to mid-August) and draws hundreds of thousands of pilgrims each year.
temples.shortDescription: A Himalayan cave shrine at 3,888 m where a naturally forming ice stalagmite is worshipped as the Swayambhu Shiva lingam, open only during the summer Yatra season.

temples.name: Arjuna’s Penance (Monolithic Relief)
temples.description: Arjuna’s Penance is the world’s largest bas-relief—carved across two massive boulders spanning 27 metres wide and 9 metres tall at Mahabalipuram, Tamil Nadu. Created in the 7th century under the Pallava dynasty, it depicts hundreds of figures—gods, humans, animals, and celestial beings—descending along a central cleft representing the Ganges River. Scholars debate whether it shows Arjuna’s penance for obtaining Shiva’s Pashupatastra weapon, or Bhagiratha’s penance to bring the Ganges to earth. Part of the UNESCO-listed Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram.
temples.shortDescription: World’s largest bas-relief at Mahabalipuram: a 27m Pallava-era carving of the Ganges Descent (or Arjuna’s Penance) with hundreds of divine and human figures.

It is on an island called Mandhata, near Khandwa city in the Narmada River at Khandwa district in Madhya Pradesh, India; the shape of the island is said to be like the Devanagari ॐ symbol.

temples.description: Bhimashankar Temple is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva situated in its eponymous village, Bhimashankar, in Pune district of Maharashtra. It is a key pilgrimage centre and contains one of the 12 Jyotirlingas. The temple's Shiva lingam is one of the three Jyotirlingas of Maharashtra. The mandir is situated on a mountain, 110 kilometers away from Pune. The temple's vicinity has rare plant and animal species. The mandir is located in Khed taluka, in the Bhimashankar forest range.

temples.name: Grishneshwar Temple
temples.description: Ghrishneshwar Jyotirlinga is a Hindu temple of Shiva in Verul village of Aurangabad district, Maharashtra, India. It is one of the 12 Jyotirlinga mandirs. The mandir is a national protected site, one and a half kilometers away from the Ellora Caves, 30 kilometres (19 miles) north-west of the city Aurangabad, and 300 kilometres (190 miles) east-northeast far from Mumbai. Ghrishneshwar is mentioned in the Shiva Purana, the Skanda Purana, the Ramayana and the Mahabharata.

temples.name: Mallikarjuna Temple
temples.description: Mallikarjuna Swamy Temple or Srisailam Temple is a Hindu temple dedicated to the deities Shiva and Parvati, located at Srisailam in the Indian state of Andhra Pradesh. It is significant to the Hindu sects of both Shaivism and Shaktism as this temple is referred to as one of the twelve Jyotirlingas of Shiva and as one of the fifty two Shakta pithas, centres of the Hindu goddess. Shiva is worshiped as Mallikarjuna and is represented by the lingam. His consort Parvati is depicted as Bhramaramba.

temples.name: Meenakshi Amman Temple
temples.description:
Meenakshi Temple, also known as Meenakshi Sundareswarar Temple, is a historic Hindu temple located on the southern bank of the Vaigai River in Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India. It is dedicated to Meenakshi, a form of Parvati, and her consort Sundareswarar (Shiva). The temple is theologically significant as it represents a confluence of various denominations of Hinduism such as Shaivism, Shaktism, and Vaishnavism.
While the Sangam literature mentions the temple city of Madurai, the existence of a temple is first referenced in the Tamil texts from 6th century CE.

It is one of the most sacred pilgrimage sites the Tirtha Kshetra for Hindus and is the first among the twelve jyotirlinga shrines of Shiva. temples.name: Trimbakeshwar Temple
temples.description: Trimbakeshwar Shiva Temple (श्री त्र्यंबकेश्वर ज्योतिर्लिंग मंदिर) is an ancient Hindu temple in the town of Trimbak, in the Trimbakeshwar tehsil, in the Nashik District of Maharashtra, India, 28 km from the city of Nashik and 40 km from Nashik road. It is dedicated to the Hindu god Shiva and is one of the twelve jyotirlingas where the Hindu genealogy registers at Trimbakeshwar, Maharashtra are kept. The origin of the sacred Godavari River is near Trimbak. Several Hindu rituals are carried out in Trimbakeshwar, for that pilgrims travel from all over India.

It is significant to the Hindu sects of Shaivism as this temple is referred to as one of the twelve Jyotirlingas.

temples.name: Jambukeswarar Temple
temples.description:
Jambukeswarar Temple, Thiruvanaikaval (also Thiruvanaikal, Jambukeswaram) is a temple of Shiva in Tiruchirapalli district, in the state of Tamil Nadu, India. It is one of the five major Shiva Temples of Tamil Nadu representing the Mahābhūta or five elements; this temple represents the element of water, or neer in Tamil. The sanctum of Jambukeswara has an underground stream.In the temple, Shiva is worshipped as Jambukeshwarar and his consort Parvati as Akilandeswari.
It is one of the 275 Paadal Petra Sthalams and has inscriptions from the Chola period.

temples.name: Group of Monuments at Pattadakal
temples.description: Pattadakal stands as the crowning achievement of early medieval Indian temple-building — the place where rival schools of architecture met and merged. Two Chalukya queens commissioned its greatest temples to immortalise their husbands' military triumphs, turning victory into stone. UNESCO hailed it as 'the apogee of an eclectic art,' a living laboratory where northern Nagara towers and southern Dravidian vimanas were built side by side, their builders consciously borrowing from each other. The Virupaksha temple, modelled on the Kailasanatha at Kanchipuram, still receives active worship. That continuum — from 8th-century queens to pilgrims today — is what makes Pattadakal irreplaceable.
temples.shortDescription: UNESCO World Heritage complex of 7th–8th century Chalukya temples blending Nagara, Dravidian, and Vesara styles, Karnataka.

temples.name: Badami Cave Temples
temples.description: The Badami cave temples are a complex of Buddhist, Hindu and Jain cave temples located in Badami, a town in the Bagalkot district in northern part of Karnataka, India. The caves are important examples of Indian rock-cut architecture, especially Badami Chalukya architecture, and the earliest date from the 6th century. Badami is a modern name and was previously known as "Vataapi", the capital of the early Chalukya dynasty, which ruled much of Karnataka from the 6th to the 8th century.

temples.name: Shore Temple
temples.description:
The Shore Temple (c. 725 AD) is a complex of temples and shrines that overlooks the shore of the Bay of Bengal. It is located in Mahabalipuram, about 60 kilometres (37 mi) south of Chennai in Tamil Nadu, India.
It is a structural temple, built with blocks of granite, dating from the 8th century AD. At the time of its creation, the site was a busy port during the reign of Narasimhavarman II of the Indian Pallava dynasty. As one of the Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram, it has been classified as a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1984.

temples.name: Pancha Rathas
temples.description: Pancha Rathas (also known as Five Rathas or Pandava Rathas or Ainthinai kovil) is a monument complex at Mahabalipuram, on the Coromandel Coast of the Bay of Bengal, in the Chengalpattu district of the state of Tamil Nadu, India. Pancha Rathas is an example of monolithic Indian rock-cut architecture. The complex was initially thought to have carved during the reign of King Narasimhavarman I (630–668 CE). However, historians such as Nagaswamy attributed all of monuments in Mahabalipuram to Narasimhavarman II (c. 690–725 CE) with the discovery of new inscriptions.

temples.name: Triveni Sangam Prayag
temples.description: At the city once called Prayaga — 'the place of sacrifice' — the sacred rivers Ganga, Yamuna, and the mythical Saraswati converge in the Triveni Sangam, the holiest confluence in Hinduism. The Ganga runs visibly clear, the Yamuna flows with a darker greenish hue, and the invisible Saraswati is said to join them from below. The Rigveda praises this meeting of rivers as a gateway to heaven. It is the principal site of the Kumbh Mela — the largest religious gathering on earth — with 660 million attendees recorded in 2025. The ashes of Mahatma Gandhi and several national leaders have been immersed here, linking sacred geography to the nation's memory.
temples.shortDescription: Where Ganga, Yamuna, and invisible Saraswati meet — the holiest confluence in Hinduism and the Kumbh Mela's sacred heart.

temples.name: Khajuraho Group of Temples
temples.description: Rising from the plains of Madhya Pradesh, Khajuraho is one of the world's great artistic enigmas — a medieval city where the sacred and sensual are inseparable. Built by the Chandela dynasty between the 9th and 11th centuries, the temples once numbered 85; roughly 25 survive today, their towering shikhara spires still intact. Their outer walls teem with thousands of carvings including the famous erotic scenes scholars read as tantric initiations — desire as a threshold on the journey inward. Inside each sanctuary the imagery gives way to stillness. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1986.
temples.shortDescription: UNESCO-listed Chandela temples in Madhya Pradesh, famed for exquisite erotic and devotional stone carvings across towering Nagara shikharas.

temples.name: Ellora Caves
temples.description: The Ellora Caves are a UNESCO World Heritage Site in Aurangabad, India. It is one of the largest rock-cut  cave complexes in the world, with artwork dating from AD 600–1000, including Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain caves. The complex is a leading example of Indian rock-cut architecture, and several are not strictly "caves" in that they have no roof.  Cave 16 features the largest single monolithic rock excavation in the world, the Kailash temple, a chariot-shaped monument dedicated to the god Shiva.

temples.name: Janaki Sthan, Sitamarhi
temples.description: Sitamarhi is revered as the birthplace of goddess Sita — the place where King Janaka found the infant Sita while ploughing a field, as told in the Valmiki Ramayana. The city's very name means 'Sita's dwelling', and Janaki Sthan marks the exact spot, drawing pilgrims for the annual Janaki Vivah ceremony that re-enacts the marriage of Rama and Sita.

temples.name: Sugandha Shakti Peetha
temples.description: Sugandha Shakti Peetha in Shikarpur, Barisal division, Bangladesh, marks where Sati's nose (sugandha, meaning fragrance) fell to earth. The presiding goddess is Sugandha and the Bhairava is Trayambaka. Located in the river delta of southern Bangladesh, the temple is one of several Shakti Peethas in present-day Bangladesh, maintained by the local Hindu minority community. The remote location in the Barisal division makes it one of the less-visited peethas on the full 51-Shakti-Peetha circuit.

temples.description: Konark Sun Temple is a 13th-century CE Hindu Sun temple at Konark about 35 kilometres (22 mi) northeast from Puri city on the coastline in Puri district, Odisha, India. The temple is attributed to king Narasingha Deva I of the Eastern Ganga dynasty about 1250 CE. Dedicated to the Hindu Sun-god Surya, it reflects the pinnacle of kalingan architecture and artistic excellence, what remains of the temple complex has the appearance of a 100-foot (30 m) high chariot with immense wheels and horses, all carved from stone.

temples.name: Modhera Sun Temple
temples.description: The  Sun Temple of Modhera is a Hindu temple dedicated to the solar deity Surya located in the village of Modhera in Mehsana district, Gujarat, India. The temple is situated on the bank of the river Pushpavati, and was constructed after 1026-1027 CE during the reign of Bhima I of the Chaulukya dynasty. The temple is no longer used for worship and is a protected monument maintained by the Archaeological Survey of India. The temple complex has three components: Gūḍhamanḍapa, the shrine hall; Sabhamanḍapa, the assembly hall and Kunḍa, the reservoir.

temples.name: Swaminarayan Akshardham
temples.description: Built over 13 years without a single piece of steel or iron, Swaminarayan Akshardham rises from 6,000 tonnes of intricately carved pink Rajasthani sandstone. The central mandir stands 108 ft tall with 17 domes, 97 carved pillars and 264 sculpted figures, all created by BAPS artisans under Pramukh Swami Maharaj and inaugurated in 1992. Spread across 23 acres, the complex combines a traditional mandir with cultural exhibitions, a water show and gardens.

temples.name: Tripura Sundari Temple
temples.description: Tripura Sundari Temple is a Hindu temple of the Goddess Tripura Sundari, better known locally as Devi Tripureshwari the third mahavidya and main form of Parvati. The temple is situated in the ancient city of Udaipur, about 55 km from Agartala, Tripura and can be reached by train and road from Agartala. It is believed to be one of the holiest Hindu shrines in this part of the country and witnesses the highest number of visitors for a temple in North-East India, after Kamakhya Temple in Assam. The state of Tripura is named after this temple.

temples.name: Ugratara Temple
temples.description: Built by Ahom King Siva Singh in 1725 AD on the western bank of the sacred Jor Pukhuri tank, Ugratara Devalaya is one of Guwahati's foremost Shakti Peethas — believed to mark where Sati's navel fell. Uniquely, the sanctum holds no idol; a small water-filled pit is worshipped as the fierce goddess Tara. Designed in the Nilachala style with a towering shikhara, the temple sustained damage in the 1897 Great Assam Earthquake and was subsequently restored.
temples.shortDescription: An Ahom-built Shakti Peetha in Guwahati where a water-filled pit, rather than any idol, is venerated as the fierce goddess Tara.

temples.name: Mithila Shakti Peetha
temples.description: Mithila Shakti Peetha, located near Janakpur in the Madhesh province of Nepal, marks where Sati's left eye fell, according to the Shakti Peetha tradition. The presiding goddess is Uma (Mahadevi) and the Bhairava is Mahakantha. Janakpur itself is the legendary birthplace of Sita (Janaki), wife of Rama, making this region doubly sacred—it lies at the intersection of Shakti tradition and the Ramayana. The Janaki Temple in Janakpur draws large pilgrimage, and this Shakti Peetha draws devotees completing the Nepalese leg of the 51-peetha circuit.

temples.name: Undavalli Caves
temples.description: Hewn directly from a sandstone hillside overlooking the Krishna River, the Undavalli Caves are one of the finest examples of early Indian rock-cut architecture. Originally a Jain retreat, the complex was adapted by Buddhist monks before evolving into a Hindu site under the Vishnukundina kings. The centrepiece is a four-storeyed cave temple — 29 m long — whose second floor shelters a magnificent 5-metre reclining Vishnu carved from a single block of granite. Pilastered facades and shrines to the Hindu Trinity make this a layered artistic landmark of the Deccan.
temples.shortDescription: A 4th–5th century CE multi-storeyed rock-cut temple complex near Vijayawada, housing a monumental reclining Vishnu carved from a single granite block.

temples.description: Varaha Cave Temple (i.e., Varaha Mandapa or the Adivaraha Cave) is a rock-cut cave temple located at Mamallapuram, on the Coromandel Coast of the Bay of Bengal in Kancheepuram District in Tamil Nadu, India. It is part of the hill top village, which is 4 kilometres (2.5 mi) to the north of the main Mahabalipurm sites of rathas and the Shore Temple. It is an example of Indian rock-cut architecture dating from the late 7th century. The temple is one of the finest testimonial to the ancient Hindu rock-cut cave architecture, out of many such caves also called mandapas.

temples.name: Krishna Mandapam
temples.description:
Mandapa of Krishna or Krishna Mandapam is a monument at Mahabalipuram, on the Coromandel Coast of the Bay of Bengal, in the Kancheepuram district of the state of Tamil Nadu, India. It is part of the Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram, a UNESCO World Heritage Site inscribed in 1984. It is located on a hillock next to the open rock relief of Descent of the Ganges (Mahabalipuram).
It is constituted by an originally open-air bas-relief dedicated to Krishna, dating to the mid-seventh century, which was later enclosed within a mandapa in the 16th century during the Vijayanagara Empire.

temples.name: Chennakesava Temple
temples.description: Consecrated in 1258 CE by Somanatha Dandanayaka, a general of Hoysala king Narasimha III, the Keshava Temple at Somanathapura represents the fully mature expression of Hoysala sacred architecture. Its trikuta (triple-shrined) layout with three towering stellate vimanas on a star-shaped jagati platform creates a visual rhythm of breathtaking complexity. Despite devastation by Malik Kafur in 1311, portions were restored under Vijayanagara and Mysuru Wodeyar patronage. Designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2023 as part of the Sacred Ensembles of the Hoysalas, it sits serenely on the banks of the Kaveri.
temples.shortDescription: Perfectly proportioned Hoysala trikuta temple on the Kaveri — among the finest examples of medieval Indian temple sculpture.

temples.name: Chennakesava Temple
temples.description: On the banks of the Yagachi River at Belur, the Chennakeshava Temple stands as one of the most extraordinary monuments of Hoysala art and faith. Commissioned in 1117 CE by King Vishnuvardhana to celebrate his victory over the Cholas at the Battle of Talakadu, it took three generations and 103 years to complete. Every surface carries intricately carved friezes narrating the Ramayana, Mahabharata, and Puranas; 118 inscriptions trace continuous patronage from the 12th century onward. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2023 under the Sacred Ensembles of the Hoysalas, it remains an active place of pilgrimage and worship.
temples.shortDescription: Hoysala masterpiece built over 103 years, its every surface narrating Hindu epics in stone — a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2023.

temples.name: Chaturbhuj Temple, Khajuraho
temples.description: Standing alone in the southern group of Khajuraho in Jatakari village, the Chaturbhuj Temple is one of the most spiritually serene shrines in the Chandela complex. Built around 1100 CE under king Yasovarman, it is uniquely distinguished as the only temple at Khajuraho entirely devoid of erotic sculpture, its walls instead carrying devotional figures and celestial imagery in quiet dignity. The sanctum houses a remarkable 2.7-metre standing Vishnu, commanding in its stillness. Part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1986, it offers unhurried contemplation rarely found in the busy western group.
temples.shortDescription: Khajuraho's only temple fully free of erotic art, housing a majestic 2.7 m Vishnu — tranquil Vaishnava devotion amid UNESCO temples.

The temple is located in Srirangam, Tiruchirapalli, Tamil Nadu, India. Constructed in the Tamil architectural style, the temple is glorified by the Tamil poet-saints called the Alvars in their canon, the Naalayira Divya Prabhandam, and has the unique distinction of being the foremost among the 108 Divya Desams dedicated to the god Vishnu.

temples.name: Badrinath Temple
temples.description: Badarinath Temple, also known as Badarinarayana Temple, is a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu. It is located in the town of Badrinath in Chamoli district of Uttarakhand, India. The temple is one of the 108 Divya Desams, sacred to Vaishnavism, where Vishnu is worshipped as Badrinath. Due to extreme weather conditions in the Himalayas, the temple is open for six months each year, from late April to early November. It is one of the most visited pilgrimage centers of India, having recorded 2.8 million (28 lakh) visits in just 2 months in 2022. It is one of the Char Dham pilgrimage sites.

temples.description:
The Padmanabhaswamy Temple (Malayalam: [pɐd̪mɐnaːbʰɐswaːmi]) is a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu in Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, India. It is one of the 108 Divya Desams, sacred sites of the Sri Vaishnava tradition.
The temple's principal deity is Anantha Padmanabhaswamy, a form of Vishnu depicted in yogic sleep, on his serpent mount named Shesha. Padmanabhaswamy is the tutelary deity of the Travancore royal family. The titular Maharaja of Travancore, Moolam Thirunal Rama Varma, is the current trustee of the temple.

The temple is dedicated to Venkateswara, a form of Vishnu, who is believed to have appeared on earth to save mankind from trials and troubles of Kali Yuga. Hence the place is also known by the name Kaliyuga Vaikuntha and the deity here is referred to as Kaliyuga Prathyaksha Daivam.
