temples.name: Naimisaranya Chakra Narayan Temple
temples.description: Deep in the ancient forest of Naimisaranya, 88,000 sages once gathered for a twelve-year yajna where Suta Goswami recited all eighteen Puranas — a moment scriptures call the cradle of Hindu sacred knowledge. At the heart of this forest stands the Naimishnath Vishnu shrine, where Lord Vishnu is said to have self-manifested. Here too lies the Chakra Tirth — a round sacred pond formed when Vishnu's Sudarshana Chakra plunged into the earth, its waters spiralling in a mystical circular flow. As Divya Desam #68, sung by Thirumangai Alvar, it holds singular sanctity in Vaishnavism.
temples.shortDescription: Swayambhu Vishnu shrine at Naimisaranya; Divya Desam #68; sacred site of Puranic recitation and the mythic Chakra Tirth whirlpool.

temples.name: Tirunarayanapuram Cheluvanarayana Swamy Temple
temples.description: Among the most venerated Abhimana Kshetras of the Srivaishnava tradition, this temple at Melukote shelters a standing six-foot form of Vishnu said to have been given by Vishnu himself to Brahma for perpetual worship. Its processional idol carries a legend unlike any other: retrieved from Delhi by Ramanujacharya, it so captivated a Muslim princess that she followed it to Melukote and merged into the divine — now enshrined as Bibi Nachiyar at the Lord's feet, honoured alongside him to this day. The deity Ramapriya is said to have been venerated by both Rama and Krishna across the yugas.
temples.shortDescription: Ancient Dravidian Vishnu temple in Melukote, Karnataka — home to a self-manifested deity and the unique Bibi Nachiyar shrine.

It is located 26 km from Tirunelveli. Constructed in the Dravidian style of architecture, the temple is glorified in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, the early medieval Tamil canon of the Alwar saints from the 6th–9th centuries CE. It is one of the 108 Divya Desams dedicated to Vishnu, who is worshipped as Adinathar and his consort Lakshmi as Adinathanayaki.

temples.name: Anantha Padmanabhaswamy Temple
temples.description: At the heart of Thiruvananthapuram rests one of India's most sacred shrines. Its deity reclines in eternal yogic sleep on Ananta the cosmic serpent — a massive murti of 12,008 shaligrama stones. Brahma blooms from Vishnu's navel, visible only through three separate doorways. Beneath lie legendary subterranean vaults: five opened by Supreme Court order in 2011 revealed an estimated $22 billion in gold and jewels, the largest such hoard in recorded history; the enigmatic Vault B remains sealed. The Travancore royal family, self-declared servants of Padmanabha, have been stewards for centuries.
temples.shortDescription: Vishnu temple in Thiruvananthapuram with a colossal reclining deity, legendary treasure vaults, and Travancore royal stewardship.

temples.name: Srimushnam Bhu Varaha Swamy Temple
temples.description: At Srimushnam stands one of the eight swayambhu Vishnu kshetras — shrines where Vishnu is believed to have self-manifested, ungiven by human hands. This is the only shrine in that sacred group where Vishnu is venerated solely in his Varaha form — the cosmic boar who plunged into primordial waters to rescue Bhu Devi from the demon Hiranyaksha. After slaying the asura, Vishnu granted his dying wish and turned southward; so the deity's boar head still gazes south while his body welcomes devotees from the west. The sweat that fell from Varaha during that cosmic battle is said to have formed the temple tank, the Nithya Pushkarani.
temples.shortDescription: One of eight swayambhu Vishnu shrines in India; the only one enshrining Varaha the boar-avatar, at Srimushnam, Cuddalore, Tamil Nadu.

temples.name: Pushkar Varaha Temple
temples.description: Perched above the sacred Pushkar Sarovar, the Varaha temple is among the oldest and most venerated shrines in Rajasthan — a towering brick-red structure rising on a plinth of thirty steps, guarded by life-size Dwarpalas and carved pillars bearing Garuda. It enshrines Varaha, Vishnu's cosmic boar avatar who plunged into the primordial ocean to rescue the Earth goddess from the demon Hiranyaksha. The surrounding lake is Tirtha-Guru — teacher of all pilgrimage waters — and Pushkar itself is Tirtha-Raja, king of sacred sites. Bathing here on Kartik Purnima is said to sever the cycle of rebirth.

temples.name: Group of Monuments at Hampi
temples.description:
Hampi or Hampe (Kannada: [hɐmpe]), also referred to as the Group of Monuments at Hampi, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site located in the town of Hampi in Vijayanagara district, east-central Karnataka, India. Hampi predates the Vijayanagara Empire; it is mentioned in the Ramayana and the Puranas of Hinduism as Pampa Devi Tirtha Kshetra. Hampi continues as a religious centre, with the Virupaksha Temple, an active Adi Shankara-linked monastery and various monuments belonging to the old city.
Hampi was the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire from 1336 to 1565 (as Vijayanagara), when it was abandoned.

temples.name: Bhaleshwor Mahadev Temple
temples.description: Bhaleshwor Mahadev is a revered Shiva temple perched at 2,551 metres atop Chandragiri Hill on the south-west rim of the Kathmandu Valley. The temple is designed by architect Shankar Nath Rimal on an ancient sacred site and connected to the valley below by a modern cable car, making it accessible while retaining its hilltop mystique. Lord Shiva is worshipped here as Bhaleshwor — master of the forehead, linked to the third eye. On clear days the panoramic view of the entire Himalayan range from Dhaulagiri to Everest is extraordinary.

temples.name: Haridwar Har Ki Pauri Temple
temples.description: Har Ki Pauri — 'the footsteps of Vishnu' — is the most sacred ghat on the Ganges in Haridwar, where the plains first receive the river descending from the Himalayas. At its heart lies the Brahmakund, believed to be the spot where drops of Amrit (the nectar of immortality) fell from Garuda's pitcher during the cosmic Samudra Manthan — making it the anchor of the Kumbh Mela. King Vikramaditya is said to have built the ghat in the 1st century BCE. Every dusk, the Ganga Aarti — lamps swirling in the hands of priests, their reflections rippling across the current — transforms the ghat into one of the most spiritually luminous spectacles on earth.
temples.shortDescription: Haridwar's holiest ghat where the Ganges meets the plains — site of the Kumbh Mela and the nightly Ganga Aarti at dusk.

temples.name: Akshardham Temple
temples.description:
Swaminarayan Akshardham is a Hindu temple and campus in Delhi, India. The temple is close to the border with Noida. Also known as Akshardham Temple or Akshardham Delhi, the complex displays traditional and modern Hindu culture and architecture. Inspired by Yogiji Maharaj and created by Pramukh Swami Maharaj, it was constructed by BAPS. It is the world's second-largest BAPS Hindu temple, following Akshardham, New Jersey, in the United States.
The temple was opened on 6 November 2005 by Pramukh Swami Maharaj in the presence of A. P. J. Abdul Kalam, Manmohan Singh, L.K Advani and B.L Joshi.

temples.description:
Yamunotri Temple is a Hindu temple, situated in the western region of Garhwal Himalayas at an altitude of 3,291 metres (10,797 ft) in Uttarkashi district, Uttarakhand, India. It's just 129 km from Uttarkashi, the main district headquarters. The temple is dedicated to Goddess Yamuna, and has a black marble idol of the goddess. 
The actual temple is only accessible by a 13 kilometres (8.1 mi) trek from the town of Hanuman Chatti and a 6 kilometres (3.7 mi) walk from Janki Chatti; horses or palanquins are available for rent.

temples.name: Palitana Temples
temples.description: Palitana on Shatrunjaya Hill, Gujarat, is the world's largest temple city—over 900 Jain temples crown the sacred hill, constructed over a thousand years from the 11th century onwards. The climb of 3,572 steps leads pilgrims past temple after temple to the main Adinatha shrine at the summit. Palitana is the holiest pilgrimage site in Jainism: every Jain aspires to complete the climb at least once. No one is permitted to remain on the hill at night—priests and pilgrims must descend each evening, leaving the gods in solitude.
temples.shortDescription: World's largest temple city—900+ Jain temples on Shatrunjaya Hill, Gujarat. A 3,572-step climb to the Adinatha summit; holiest Jain pilgrimage, no overnight stay permitted.

temples.name: Girnar Jain Temples
temples.description: Girnar Hill near Junagadh, Gujarat, rises to 1,117 metres and hosts one of Jainism's most sacred pilgrimage sites—dedicated to Neminatha, the 22nd Tirthankara, who is said to have attained nirvana here. The Jain temples cluster from 600m altitude upwards, culminating at the Neminatha temple near the summit, reached by over 10,000 steps. The same hill also hosts important Hindu shrines including Ambaji and Gorakhnath, making Girnar an extraordinary multi-faith sacred mountain shared by Jains, Hindus, and Nath yogis.
temples.shortDescription: Sacred Jain pilgrimage hill near Junagadh with 10,000+ steps to the Neminatha temple. Also home to Hindu shrines; one of Gujarat's holiest multi-faith mountains.

temples.name: Taranga Jain Temple
temples.description: Taranga Jain Temple on Taranga Hill, Mehsana district, Gujarat, is one of the finest examples of Solanki (Chaulukya) architectural achievement in Jain temple building. The main Ajitanatha temple, dedicated to the 2nd Tirthankara, was built in the 12th century under King Kumarapala of the Solanki dynasty. The temple's ornate white marble shikhara, intricate pillared halls, and delicately carved ceilings represent the apex of medieval Gujarati craftsmanship. The hilltop setting, accessible by road, draws Jain pilgrims and architecture enthusiasts alike.
temples.shortDescription: Solanki-era Jain temple on Taranga Hill, Gujarat, dedicated to Ajitanatha. Built under King Kumarapala in the 12th century; exemplary white marble Solanki architecture.

festivals.name: Paryushana
festivals.description: Jain fasting and introspection festival drawing large pilgrimage to Taranga Hill.

temples.name: Bhadreshwar Jain Temple
temples.description: Bhadreshwar Jain Temple in Kutch, Gujarat, claims to be among the oldest Jain pilgrimage sites in India, with a lineage of temples at this location dating back over 2,500 years according to Jain tradition. The current temple complex, dedicated to Parshvanatha, was largely rebuilt in the 12th century under Solanki patronage after earlier structures were destroyed. Remotely situated on the Kutch plains, Bhadreshwar (also called Vashishtha) represents the westernmost major Jain pilgrimage site in India.
temples.shortDescription: One of India's oldest Jain pilgrimage sites in Kutch, Gujarat—Parshvanatha temple rebuilt in the 12th century under Solanki patronage; westernmost major Jain tirth.

festivals.name: Paryushana
festivals.description: Eight-day Jain fasting festival drawing Kutchi Jain community and pilgrims from Bhuj and beyond.

temples.name: Kumbharia Jain Temples
temples.description: Kumbharia Jain Temples near Ambaji, Banaskantha, Gujarat, are a cluster of five Jain temples built in the 11th–12th century under Solanki patronage, renowned for their exceptionally intricate white marble carvings. The main Mahavira temple and the Parshvanatha temple display the finest surviving examples of Solanki decorative carving—floral lattices, celestial dancers, and mythological scenes of extraordinary delicacy. Though less famous than Dilwara (Mount Abu), many art historians consider Kumbharia's carving quality comparable.
temples.shortDescription: Five Solanki-era Jain temples near Ambaji with white marble carvings rivalling Dilwara. 11th–12th century masterworks of Gujarati stone craftsmanship.

festivals.name: Paryushana
festivals.description: Eight-day Jain fasting and worship festival at Kumbharia.

temples.name: Jagdish Temple
temples.description: Jagdish Temple in Udaipur, built in 1651 by Maharana Jagat Singh I of the Mewar dynasty, is the largest and most important temple in Udaipur. Standing just north of the City Palace, the three-storey Indo-Aryan shikhara towers over the old city, visible from most of Lake Pichola. The sanctum holds a black stone image of Vishnu as Lord of the Universe (Jagannath). The exterior is richly carved with elephants, horsemen, celestial dancers, and scenes from the epics—a fine specimen of late Rajput temple architecture with Solanki influences.
temples.shortDescription: Udaipur's largest temple, built 1651 by Maharana Jagat Singh I, with a soaring Indo-Aryan shikhara above the City Palace. Black stone Vishnu idol; richly carved exterior.

festivals.name: Janmashtami
festivals.description: Birth anniversary of Krishna celebrated with midnight worship, abhisheka and devotional singing.

festivals.name: Holi
festivals.description: Festival of colours celebrated with royal Mewar traditions; the temple and surrounding ghats come alive.

temples.name: Hutheesing Jain Temple
temples.description: Hutheesing Jain Temple in Ahmedabad, built in 1848 by wealthy Jain merchant Hutheesing Kesarising during a severe famine (thereby providing employment), is one of the finest examples of Jain marble architecture in India. Dedicated to Dharmanatha, the 15th Tirthankara, the temple features an elaborately carved white marble complex with 52 shrines, a multi-storey rang mandap, and extraordinarily detailed lattice work. Though built in the 19th century, the craftsmanship follows the classical Solanki Jain tradition in material and ornament.
temples.shortDescription: Ahmedabad's finest Jain marble temple, built 1848 by merchant Hutheesing to provide famine relief employment. 52 shrines, elaborate Solanki-style white marble carving.

festivals.name: Paryushana
festivals.description: Jain eight-day fasting festival with elaborate rituals in this elaborate marble temple.

temples.name: Kiradu Temples
temples.description: Kiradu, 35 km from Barmer in the Thar Desert, is known as the 'Khajuraho of Rajasthan' for its cluster of five 11th–12th century Solanki-style temples, ornately carved in the hard local sandstone. The largest, the Someshvara temple, features exquisitely detailed sculpture—celestial dancers, erotic carvings, mythological scenes—comparable in quality to Khajuraho but far less visited. The remote desert location gives the ruins a haunting quality; local legend holds the village was cursed and abandoned. The Archaeological Survey of India maintains the site.
temples.shortDescription: 'Khajuraho of Rajasthan'—five Solanki-era temples 35km from Barmer in the Thar Desert, with intricate 11th-century sandstone carving. Remote and atmospheric desert ruin.

festivals.name: Mahashivratri
festivals.description: Shiva festival drawing local pilgrims from Barmer district to the Someshvara temple ruins.

temples.name: Navlakha Temple
temples.description: Navlakha Temple at Ghumli in Devbhumi Dwarka, Gujarat, is a ruined but architecturally magnificent 11th–12th century Solanki-era temple said to have cost nine lakh coins (nav-lakha) to build. Though now in ruins, the surviving carved stonework—particularly the ornate shikhara fragments and carved pillars—reveals the exceptional quality of Solanki craftsmanship. Ghumli was once the capital of the Jethwa Rajput chiefs and a wealthy port town; the Navlakha temple was its crowning monument. The site offers superb views of the Saurashtra coastline.
temples.shortDescription: Ruined 11th-century Solanki temple at Ghumli, Gujarat, said to have cost nine lakh coins. Surviving carved stonework shows Solanki artistry at its finest; coastal views.

festivals.name: Mahashivratri
festivals.description: Shiva festival at this historic site; local Jethwa community observes special puja.

temples.name: Tiru Parameswara Vinnagaram
temples.description: Tiru Parameswara Vinnagaram (Vaikunta Perumal Temple) in Kanchipuram is one of the earliest and most historically significant Pallava temples, built by Nandivarman II (Pallavamalla) in the 8th century CE. Uniquely, it contains three sanctums stacked vertically—Vishnu is enshrined in standing, seated, and reclining postures at ground, first, and second storeys. The temple's exterior sculptural programme depicts the history of the Pallava dynasty in stone reliefs—making it the most important historical document of Pallava dynastic history. It is both a Divya Desam and a key monument in Pallava art.
temples.shortDescription: 8th-century Pallava temple in Kanchipuram with three stacked Vishnu sanctums (standing, seated, reclining). Exterior reliefs are the primary stone record of Pallava dynastic history.

festivals.name: Vaikunta Ekadashi
festivals.description: The most sacred Vaishnava day when the Paramapada Vasal (Gateway to Heaven) is opened; massive crowds at this Divya Desam.

festivals.name: Brahmotsavam
festivals.description: Annual grand festival of this Divya Desam temple with processions and classical music.

temples.name: Kukkuteswara Swami Temple
temples.description: The Kukkuteswara Swami Temple at Pitapuram, Andhra Pradesh, is one of the 18 Maha Shakti Peethas — the most exalted tier of Shakti pilgrimage. The presiding deity is Kukkuteswara Shiva (symbolised as a rooster/cock), paired with goddess Puruhutika. Pitapuram is also the birthplace of Gaudapada, the teacher of Adi Shankaracharya's own guru. The sacred Kumara Pushkarini tank draws pilgrims for ritual bathing before darshan. Both Shaiva and Shakta traditions hold this site in the highest reverence.
temples.shortDescription: One of the 18 Maha Shakti Peethas — Kukkuteswara Shiva and Puruhutika Devi in Pitapuram, birthplace of Gaudapada.

festivals.name: Navaratri
festivals.description: Grand Shakti celebrations at this Maha Shakti Peetha drawing pilgrims from across Andhra Pradesh.

festivals.name: Mahashivaratri
festivals.description: Night-long Shiva worship at Kukkuteswara with special abhisheka.

temples.name: Danteshwari Temple
temples.description: The Danteshwari Temple at Dantewada, Chhattisgarh, is one of the 52 Shakti Peethas, built in the 14th century by Kakatiya rulers. The goddess Danteshwari — patroness of the Bastar kingdom — gives the town its name. The temple stands at the confluence of Shankini and Dankini rivers in a densely forested tribal region. Bastar's Dussehra, one of India's longest festivals (75 days), centres on this goddess — her chariot procession is a spectacle unlike any other in the country.
temples.shortDescription: 14th-century Shakti Peetha patroness of the Bastar kingdom — Danteshwari Devi at the centre of Bastar's unique 75-day Dussehra.

festivals.name: Bastar Dussehra
festivals.description: India's longest Dussehra festival (75 days) revolves around Danteshwari Devi's grand chariot procession.

festivals.name: Navaratri
festivals.description: Special Shakti worship at the temple with tribal community participation.

temples.name: Dhakeshwari National Temple
temples.description: Dhakeshwari National Temple in Old Dhaka is Bangladesh's sole state-owned Hindu temple — the only Muslim-majority nation to designate a National Hindu Temple. As one of the 51 Shakti Peethas, it marks where Sati's crown jewel fell. "Dhakeshwari" — Goddess of Dhaka — is believed to be the origin of the city's name itself. The temple was damaged in the 1971 Liberation War but rebuilt, becoming a powerful symbol of Bangladesh's religious pluralism. Its Durga Puja is the largest in Bangladesh.
temples.shortDescription: Bangladesh's National Hindu Temple and Shakti Peetha — Goddess Dhakeshwari gave Dhaka its name; the nation's most important Hindu site.

festivals.name: Durga Puja
festivals.description: The largest Durga Puja in Bangladesh — celebrated with great community fervour at the National Temple.

festivals.name: Navaratri
festivals.description: Nine nights of Shakti worship drawing Hindus from across Bangladesh.

temples.name: Tarapith Temple
temples.description: Tarapith in Birbhum, West Bengal, is among India's most powerful tantric pilgrimage sites. The temple is dedicated to Goddess Tara — the second of the Dasha Mahavidya — and its adjoining shmashana (cremation ground) is used for tantric sadhana as much as the sanctum. The 19th-century saint Bamakhepa, the "mad saint of Tarapith," practised here and embodies the temple's mystical spirit. Pilgrims come seeking liberation, tantric power and Tara's fierce maternal grace. The site pulses with an intensity found in few temples.
temples.shortDescription: Birbhum's most powerful tantric site — Goddess Tara and the sacred cremation ground where saint Bamakhepa attained realisation.

festivals.name: Kali Puja
festivals.description: The most intense festival — tantric rituals performed at the temple and cremation ground through the night.

festivals.name: Navaratri
festivals.description: Nine nights of Tara Devi worship with special tantric pujas and thousands of pilgrims.

temples.name: Aranya Devi Temple, Arrah
temples.description: The Aranya Devi Temple at Arrah, Bihar, is the presiding deity and cultural heart of the city — the name "Ara" (Arrah) is itself derived from the goddess Aranya Devi. As goddess of the forest, Aranya Devi is the protector of the entire Bhojpur region and its Kuldevi. One of the Shakti Peethas, the temple draws vast numbers during festivals when the entire population of Arrah participates in communal worship. The city's identity has been inseparable from this temple for centuries.
temples.shortDescription: Arrah's patron goddess — Aranya Devi gave the city its name and serves as Kuldevi of the entire Bhojpur region.

festivals.name: Navaratri
festivals.description: The entire city of Arrah gathers for nine nights of communal worship at the patron goddess temple.

festivals.name: Chaitra Navaratri
festivals.description: Spring Navaratri with special aarti and community celebration.

temples.name: Chandranath Temple
temples.description: Chandranath Temple perches at 1,020 feet atop Chandranath Hill near Chittagong, Bangladesh — the highest point in the area and the most sacred Hindu pilgrimage site in Bangladesh. As one of the 51 Shakti Peethas, it marks where the right arm of Sati fell. The steep forest ascent passes dozens of small shrines, making the climb itself an act of devotion. The panoramic view over Chittagong and the Bay of Bengal from the summit is extraordinary. The annual Shivaratri fair here is one of Bangladesh's largest Hindu gatherings.
temples.shortDescription: Highest point in Bangladesh and most sacred Hindu pilgrimage site — Shakti Peetha where Sati's right arm fell, with panoramic Bay of Bengal views.

festivals.name: Mahashivaratri
festivals.description: Bangladesh's largest Hindu pilgrimage gathering — lakhs climb Chandranath Hill for Shiva worship.

festivals.name: Navaratri
festivals.description: Nine nights of Bhavani Shakti worship at this hilltop Peetha.

temples.name: Jnanakshi Rajarajeshwari Temple
temples.description: The Jnanakshi Rajarajeshwari Temple in Bengaluru, founded in 1978 by Sri Sivaratnapuri Swamiji, enshrines Goddess Rajarajeshwari — the supreme form of Lalita Tripurasundari worshipped with Srichakra rituals. The temple is considered one of the Shakti Peethas, representing the "ear" (karnabhaga) of Bharatamata. Navaratri here is an elaborate nine-day event drawing devotees from across Bengaluru. The Srichakra Puja performed daily is considered one of the most powerful forms of Shakti worship.
temples.shortDescription: Bengaluru Shakti Peetha enshrining Rajarajeshwari as Lalita Tripurasundari — the "ear of Bharatamata" with elaborate daily Srichakra worship.

festivals.name: Navaratri
festivals.description: Nine-day elaborate Shakti celebration with daily alankara and Srichakra puja, drawing thousands of Bengaluru devotees.

festivals.name: Varalakshmi Vratam
festivals.description: Special Lakshmi puja with Srichakra rituals at the goddess temple.

temples.name: Alopi Devi Mandir
temples.description: Alopi Devi Mandir at Alopibagh, Prayagraj, stands near the holy Sangam (confluence of Ganga, Yamuna and mythical Sarasvati). As one of the 51 Shakti Peethas, it marks where Sati's palanquin (doli) fell — and uniquely, the temple enshrines no idol but an empty wooden palanquin (rath) as the symbol of the goddess, representing her disappearance (alopi means "vanished"). During Kumbh Mela, millions visit this temple. The Maratha leader Mahadji Shinde developed the Sangam site in 1771.
temples.shortDescription: Prayagraj Shakti Peetha near the Sangam — uniquely worships an empty palanquin (the goddess vanished here); vital Kumbh Mela pilgrimage stop.

festivals.name: Kumbh Mela
festivals.description: Every 12 years, tens of millions visit this temple as part of the Prayagraj Kumbh pilgrimage circuit.

festivals.name: Navaratri
festivals.description: Nine nights of Shakti worship at this unique imageless Peetha near the sacred Sangam.
